---
title: "Workflow"
---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| error: false
library(tidyverse)
library(tibble)
library(kableExtra)
```

This page describes the data management of this work, from the pre-processing steps to the results. The goal is to write step by step a replication package. However, note that some data are not public and should be required to their owners (namely the LIFFULL HOMES dataset the panel dataset from Keio University).

Code used in this section: `Python`, `SQL` and `Stata`.

```{python}
#| eval: false
import pandas as pd
import duckdb
```


## CLEANING

### Let's clean the Liffull Homes datasets

The Liffull Homes data consists of actually two types of datasets. The first one, that I will call "monthly", includes all the listings from July 2015 to June 2017. The second one, "period", includes all the listings from October 2018 to December 2014. Unfortunately, those two do not match perfectly well.

Let's begin with the "monthly". This one is provided as 24 .tsv files, for each year from July 2015 to June 2017. First, let's append them. Since they are huge files (around 70GB), I proceed using Python and the Duckdb library:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Get the new column names from the codebook
names_codebook = pd.read_excel("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/data_spec_monthly_20181113.xlsx", 
                               sheet_name=1, 
                               skiprows=2)
names_new = names_codebook["フィールド名"].tolist() # フィールド名 is the list of variable names in English

# Append
duckdb.sql(f"""
    COPY (
        SELECT * FROM read_csv_auto(
            '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/*.tsv',
            sep='\t',
            union_by_name=true,
            names={names_new}
        )
    ) TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents1_combined.parquet'
    (FORMAT PARQUET, COMPRESSION 'zstd')
""")
```

Many of the columns which can be discarded and I create a lighter parquet file:

```{=html}
<style>
.code-preview {
  max-height: 200px;
  overflow: hidden;
  position: relative;
}
.code-preview::after {
  content: "";
  position: absolute;
  bottom: 0;
  left: 0;
  right: 0;
  height: 80px;
  background: linear-gradient(transparent, var(--bs-body-bg, white));
  pointer-events: none;
}
.code-preview.expanded {
  max-height: none;
}
.code-preview.expanded::after {
  display: none;
}
.expand-btn {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;
  align-items: center;
  width: 100%;
  margin: -10px 0 12px;
  cursor: pointer;
  background: none;
  border: none;
  padding: 4px 0;
  position: relative;
  top: 8px;
}
.expand-btn svg {
  width: 96px;
  height: 32px;
  transition: transform 0.3s ease;
}
.expand-btn.expanded svg {
  transform: rotate(180deg);
}
</style>

<script>
document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function () {
  var ns = "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg";

  document.querySelectorAll(".fold-chunk").forEach(function (el) {
    var wrapper = document.createElement("div");
    wrapper.className = "code-preview";
    el.parentNode.insertBefore(wrapper, el);
    wrapper.appendChild(el);

    var svg = document.createElementNS(ns, "svg");
    svg.setAttribute("viewBox", "0 0 72 24");

    var poly = document.createElementNS(ns, "polyline");
    poly.setAttribute("points", "4,8 36,20 68,8");
    poly.setAttribute("stroke", "#888888");
    poly.setAttribute("stroke-width", "3");
    poly.setAttribute("stroke-linecap", "round");
    poly.setAttribute("stroke-linejoin", "round");
    poly.setAttribute("fill", "none");

    svg.appendChild(poly);

    var btn = document.createElement("button");
    btn.className = "expand-btn";
    btn.appendChild(svg);
    wrapper.parentNode.insertBefore(btn, wrapper.nextSibling);

    btn.addEventListener("click", function () {
      wrapper.classList.toggle("expanded");
      btn.classList.toggle("expanded");
    });
  });
});
</script>
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| class-source: fold-chunk
con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT
        bukken_id::VARCHAR AS id,
        start_date::DATE AS start_date,
        end_date::DATE AS end_date,
        bukken_type::BIGINT AS type,
        addr1_1::BIGINT AS prefecture,
        addr1_2::BIGINT AS city_district,
        eki1::BIGINT AS eki1,
        bus_time1::BIGINT AS bus1_time,
        walk_distance1::BIGINT AS station1_distance,
        bus_time2::BIGINT AS bus2_time,
        walk_distance2::BIGINT AS station2_distance,
        land_youto::BIGINT AS land_use_zone,
        land_toshi::BIGINT AS urban_plannning,
        land_chisei::BIGINT AS topography,
        land_area::DOUBLE AS land_area,
        land_shidou::DOUBLE AS private_road_area,
        land_setback_flg::BIGINT AS setback_y,
        land_kenpei::BIGINT AS building_coverage_ratio,
        land_youseki::BIGINT AS floor_area_ratio,
        land_right::BIGINT as land_right,
        land_kokudohou::BIGINT AS land_use_planning,
        house_kouzou::BIGINT AS building_structure,
        house_area::DOUBLE AS sqft,
        house_kaisuu::BIGINT AS building_nb_of_floors,
        house_kaisuu_chika::BIGINT,
        kenchiku_date::BIGINT AS construction_date,
        flg_new::BIGINT AS new_y,
        room_kaisuu::BIGINT AS floor,
        balcony_area::DOUBLE AS balcony_area,
        madori_number_all::BIGINT AS rooms,
        madori_kind_all::BIGINT AS floor_plan_type,
        money_room::BIGINT AS price1,
        money_tsubo::BIGINT AS price_tsubo,
        money_kyoueki::BIGINT AS common_area_fee,
        money_combo::BIGINT AS price,
        parking_kubun::BIGINT AS parking_y,
        school_ele_distance::BIGINT AS school_ele_distance,
        school_jun_distance::BIGINT AS school_jun_distance,
        convenience_distance::BIGINT AS convenience_distance,
        super_distance::BIGINT AS super_distance,
        hospital_distance::BIGINT AS hospital_distance,
        nl::DOUBLE AS lat,
        el::DOUBLE AS lon
    FROM read_parquet(
        '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_combined.parquet'
    )
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_renamed.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
"""
)
```

Now, to get a event lighter dataset, I drop where `money_room`and `house_aear`/ `land_area` are missing. Taking a step forward to the sample selection, I keep only land for sales, condominium or apartments.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "See property type codebook"
/*
From the codebook:
1101 Land for Sale
1102 Transfer of Leasehold Rights
1103 Transfer of Underlying Land Rights
1201 Newly Built Detached House
1202 Used Detached House
1203 Newly Built Terrace House
1204 Used Terrace House
1301 Newly Built Apartment
1302 Used Apartment
1303 Newly Built Public Housing
1304 Used Public Housing
1305 Newly Built Public Housing
1306 Used Public Housing
1307 Newly Built Townhouse
1308 Used Townhouse
1309 Resort Apartment
1401 Shop
1403 Shop with Residence
1404 Residence with Shop
1405 Office
1406 Shop/Office
1407 Building
1408 Factory
1409 Apartment
1410 Warehouse
1411 Apartment
1412 Dormitory
1413 Inn
1414 Hotel
1415 Vacation home
1416 Resort Mansion
1420 Company housing
1499 Other
1502 Shop
1505 Office
1506 Shop/Office
1507 Building
1509 Condominium
1599 Other
3101 Condominium
3102 Apartment
3103 Detached house
3104 Terrace house
3105 Townhouse
3106 Room rental
3110 Dormitory/Boarding house
3201 Shop (entire building)
3202 Shop (part of building)
3203 Office
3204 Shop/Office
3205 Factory
3206 Warehouse
3207 Detached house
3208 Condominium
3209 Inn
3210 Dormitory
3211 Vacation home
3212 Land
3213 Building
3214 Shop with residence (detached house)
3215 Residential property with commercial space (part of the building)
3231 Store
3282 Parking lot
3299 Other
*/
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT *
    FROM read_parquet(
        '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_renamed.parquet'
    )
    WHERE
        price IS NOT NULL AND price != 0
    AND (
        (sqft IS NOT NULL AND sqft != 0)
        OR
        (land_area IS NOT NULL AND land_area != 0)
    )
        AND type IN (1101, 1201, 1202, 1203, 1204, 3101, 3102, 3103, 3104, 3105, 3212)
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_filtered.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
""")
```

It is now possible to merge this dataset with the J-SHIS hazard maps.Here they are:

```{python}
#| eval: false
shapefile_map = {
    2014: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2015: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2016: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2016-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2016-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2017: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2017-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2017-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2018: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2018-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2018-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2019: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2019-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2019-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2020: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2020-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2020-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2021: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2021-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2021-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2022: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2023: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2024: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp'
}
```

You might notice that for years 2015, 2023 and 2024, the associated shapefiles covers some other years. The reason is that they do not exist. I decide to take the previous risk map for 2014 and the 2022 for 2013 and 2024, acknowledging that this is a lesser evil in my research design, but arguing that this should not be harmful since those maps, even though evolving year by year, are computed using a Poisson process over decades and do not move that much from one year to another.

The important thing to pay attention to is to watch out for CRS issue. The coodebook says that the data are EPSG:4326 encoded. However, the maps are EPSG:4612. Those two differ only by a few meters but still let's convert during merging.

To merge, I use once again `SQL`:

```{python}
#| eval: false
con.execute("INSTALL spatial;")
con.execute("LOAD spatial;")
```

To optimize performance, I load the data and proceed with subset for each year:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# =========================================================
# 1. LOAD RENTS1
# =========================================================
con.execute("""
CREATE TABLE rents1 AS
SELECT
    *,
    YEAR(start_date) AS year
FROM read_parquet(
    '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_filtered.parquet'
)
""")

# =========================================================
# 2. LOOP OVER YEARS
# =========================================================
years = range(2014, 2018)

for year in years:

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE rents1_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM rents1
        WHERE year = {year}
          AND lat IS NOT NULL
          AND lon IS NOT NULL
    """)

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE map_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM ST_Read('{shapefile_map[year]}')
    """)

    # SPATIAL MERGE
    con.execute(f"""
    CREATE TABLE rents1_{year}_geo AS
    SELECT
        r.*,
        s.CODE        AS mesh_code,
        s.T30_I45_PS,
        s.T30_I50_PS,
        s.T30_I55_PS,
        s.T30_I60_PS
    FROM rents1_{year} r
    LEFT JOIN map_{year} s
        ON ST_Within(
            ST_Transform(ST_Point(r.lon, r.lat), 'EPSG:4326', 'EPSG:4612'),
            s.geom
        )
""")

    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE rents1_{year}")
    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE map_{year}")
```

All the rents1_$*$ remain to be appended:

```{r}
#| eval: false
con.execute("""
CREATE TABLE rents_geo AS
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2014_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2015_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2016_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2017_geo
""")
```

Finally, let's save this in a `rents1.parquet`file.

```{python}
#| eval: false
con.execute("""
COPY rents_geo TO
    '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/processed/rents1.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET)
""")
```

Now I have processed with "monthly", let's clean the "period" data. Those ones cover the listings from October 2018 to November 2024. Two datasets are attached: the first one is called "bukken_rent_v1.0.1" and deals with rents, and the second one, "bukken_sale_v1.0.1", is about the sales.

Two main differences: the names and the CRS. Let's easily rename the rents2 dataset as the rents1 dataset while selecting the associated columns.

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| class-source: fold-chunk
con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT
        id::VARCHAR AS id,
        pub_start_date::DATE AS start_date,
        pub_end_date::DATE AS end_date,
        bukken_type::BIGINT AS type,
        addr1_1::BIGINT AS prefecture,
        addr1_2::BIGINT AS city_district,
        eki1::BIGINT AS eki1,
        bus_time1::BIGINT AS bus1_time,
        walk_distance1::BIGINT AS station1_distance,
        bus_time2::BIGINT AS bus2_time,
        walk_distance2::BIGINT AS station2_distance,
        land_youto::BIGINT AS land_use_zone,
        land_toshi::BIGINT AS urban_plannning,
        land_chisei::BIGINT AS topography,
        land_area::DOUBLE AS land_area,
        land_shidou::DOUBLE AS private_road_area,
        land_setback_flg::BIGINT AS setback_y,
        land_kenpei::BIGINT AS building_coverage_ratio,
        land_youseki::BIGINT AS floor_area_ratio,
        land_right::BIGINT AS land_right,
        land_kokudohou::BIGINT AS land_use_planning,
        house_kouzou::BIGINT AS building_structure,
        house_area::DOUBLE AS sqft,
        house_kaisuu::BIGINT AS building_nb_of_floors,
        house_kaisuu_chika::BIGINT AS house_kaisuu_chika,
        kenchiku_date::BIGINT AS construction_date,
        flg_new::BIGINT AS new_y,
        room_kaisuu::BIGINT AS floor,
        balcony_area::DOUBLE AS balcony_area,
        madori_number_all::BIGINT AS rooms,
        madori_kind_all::BIGINT AS floor_plan_type,
        money_room::BIGINT AS price1,
        money_tsubo::BIGINT AS price_tsubo,
        money_kyoueki::BIGINT AS common_area_fee,
        money_combo::BIGINT AS price,
        parking_kubun::BIGINT AS parking_y,
        school_ele_distance::BIGINT AS school_ele_distance,
        school_jun_distance::BIGINT AS school_jun_distance,
        convenience_distance::BIGINT AS convenience_distance,
        super_distance::BIGINT AS super_distance,
        hospital_distance::BIGINT AS hospital_distance,
        nl::DOUBLE AS lat,
        el::DOUBLE AS lon
    FROM read_csv_auto('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents_2024/bukken_rent_v1.0.1.tsv', delim='\t', ignore_errors=true)
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents2_renamed.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
""")
```

I apply the same sample selection as rents1

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| echo: false
con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT *
    FROM read_parquet(
        '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents2_renamed.parquet'
    )
    WHERE
        price IS NOT NULL AND price != 0
    AND (
        (sqft IS NOT NULL AND sqft != 0)
        OR
        (land_area IS NOT NULL AND land_area != 0)
    )
        AND type IN (1101, 1201, 1202, 1203, 1204, 3101, 3102, 3103, 3104, 3105, 3212)
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents2_filtered.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
""")
```

Let's tackle the second issue: the CRS. This has been challenging but finally break down. `lon` and `lat` are encoded differently than in rents1. After some research, lat and lon are in  

```{python}
#| eval: false
con.execute("""
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE rents2 AS
SELECT
    * EXCLUDE (lat, lon),
    YEAR(start_date) AS year,
    lat / 3600000.0 AS lat,
    lon / 3600000.0 AS lon
FROM '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents2_filtered.parquet';
""")
```

Using the same loop as before, I merge rents2 with the years following:

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| class-source: fold-chunk
# =========================================================
#  LOOP OVER YEARS
# =========================================================
years = range(2018, 2024)

for year in years:

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE rents2_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM rents2
        WHERE year = {year}
          AND lat IS NOT NULL
          AND lon IS NOT NULL
    """)

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE map_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM ST_Read({shapefile_map})
    """)

    # SPATIAL MERGE
    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE rents2_{year}_geo AS
        SELECT
            r.*,
            ST_Transform(
                ST_Point(r.lon, r.lat),
                'EPSG:4326',
                'EPSG:4612'
            ) AS point_geom,
            s.CODE        AS mesh_code,
            s.T30_I45_PS,
            s.T30_I50_PS,
            s.T30_I55_PS,
            s.T30_I60_PS
        FROM rents2_{year} r
        LEFT JOIN map_{year} s
            ON ST_Within(
                ST_Transform(ST_Point(r.lon, r.lat), 'EPSG:4326', 'EPSG:4612'),
                s.geom
            )
    """)

    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE rents2_{year}")
    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE map_{year}")
    
# =========================================================
#  APPEND
# =========================================================

con.execute("""
CREATE TABLE rents_geo AS
    SELECT * FROM rents_2018_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2019_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2020_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2021_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2022_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2023_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2024_geo
""")

con.execute("""
COPY rents_geo TO
    '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents2.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET)
""")
```

With a few name exceptions, the last dataset ("bukken_sale_v1.0.1") follows the same logic and I end up with three datasets that I finally append.

```{python}
#| eval: false
# =========================================================
#  APPEND THE THREE LIFULL HOMES DATASETS
# =========================================================

con.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE data_all AS
    SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents1.parquet')
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents2.parquet')
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/sales.parquet')
""")

# This is intermediate step to discard potential duplicates
con.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE clean_data AS
    SELECT DISTINCT * FROM data_all
""")

con.execute("""
    COPY clean_data
    TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/liffull.parquet'
    (FORMAT PARQUET)
""")

con.execute("DROP TABLE raw_data")
con.execute("DROP TABLE clean_data")
```

### Let's clean the earthquakes records from the JMA

Now, let's turn to the JMA files. They can be download for each year and contain every single earthquake records. I download them for years from 2015 to 2024 and I use first Python and second Stata to clean and select them.

```{python}
#| eval: false
import pandas as pd
import glob
```

First, I append them.

```{python}
#| eval: false
files = glob.glob("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/h*")

# First, I convert the files in txt format
def convert_file_to_txt(file_input, file_output):
    with open(file_input, "r", encoding = "utf-8") as f:
        lines = f.readlines()

    with open (file_output, "w", encoding = "utf-8") as f:
        for line in lines:
            f.write(line)
            
for i, file in enumerate(files):
    year = 2015 + i
    convert_file_to_txt(file, f"/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_{year}.txt")
 
# Now, I append   
files_txt = glob.glob("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_*.txt")
files_txt.sort()

with open("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for file in files_txt:
        with open(file, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f1:
            f.write(f1.read())
```

Second, I use the documentation provided by the JMA (https://www.data.jma.go.jp/eqev/data/bulletin/data/shindo/format_e.txt) to parse the lines.

```{python}
#| eval: false
def parse_line(line):
    try:
        return {
            "date":             f"{line[1:5]}-{line[5:7]}-{line[7:9]}",
            "magnitude":        line[52:54].strip(),
            "max_intensity":    line[61].strip(),
            # The CRS is once again different. The following formula converts from DMS to WGS84 (epsg:4326) format.
            "eq_lat":           int(line[21:24]) + (int(line[24:28]) / 100) / 60,
            "eq_lon":           int(line[32:36]) + (int(line[36:40]) / 100) / 60,
            "damage_class":     line[62].strip(),
            "tsunami_class":    line[63].strip(),
            "district_number":  line[64].strip(),
            "region_number":    line[65:68].strip(),
        }
    except:
        return None

with open("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

data = [parsed for line in lines if (parsed := parse_line(line)) is not None]

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df.to_stata("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.dta", write_index=False)
```

Switching in Stata, I proceed to the sample selection when it comes to the earthquakes.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
use "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.dta", clear

* DROP
drop if inlist(magnitude, "A0", "A1", "A2", "A3", "A4")
destring magnitude, replace
drop if magnitude == .
drop if magnitude < 50

* KEEP only the seisms with max_intensity >= 5
keep if inlist(max_intensity, "7", "A", "B", "C", "D")
replace max_intensity = "5"   if max_intensity == "A"
replace max_intensity = "5.5" if max_intensity == "B"
replace max_intensity = "6"   if max_intensity == "C"
replace max_intensity = "6.5" if max_intensity == "D"
destring max_intensity, replace

* Standardize magnitude
replace magnitude = magnitude / 10

* Destring other variables
destring damage_class tsunami_class, replace
replace damage_class  = 0 if damage_class  == .
replace tsunami_class = 0 if tsunami_class == .
destring district_number, replace

* For the seisms occuring the same day, keep only the strongest one
bysort date: keep if _n == _N

* Create date
gen eq_date = date(date, "YMD")
drop date

* Preparing an index for the future merge
gen eq_index = _n

* Get it in the right order and save
order eq_date eq_index magnitude max_intensity eq_lat eq_lon damage_class tsunami_class district_number region_number
save "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/eq_records.dta", replace
```

### Let's clean data from other control variables

To capture the attractiveness of a property, I collect other data from e-stat and some other macroeconomic variables.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
********************************************************************************
* MERGE RAW FILES

local files pop adm lab edu dwe med cul
local pop_opts `" clear "'
local other_opts `" rowrange(9) varnames(9) clear "'

* Load population first
import delimited using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/e-stat/pop.csv", clear
drop if year < 2015
tempfile estat_combined
save `estat_combined', replace emptyok

* Merge with the rest of the datasets
foreach f in adm lab edu dwe med cul {
    import delimited using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/e-stat/`f'.csv", ///
        rowrange(9) varnames(9) clear
    drop items
    drop if year < 2015
    merge 1:1 year areacode using `estat_combined', nogenerate
    save `estat_combined', replace
}

* Convert to numeric
use `estat_combined', clear
destring g1201_numberofcommunitycentersnu - a7101_numberofhouseholdstotalhou, ///
    replace ignore("***" "-" ",")
```

Many values are missing. For example, the variables from the "population" category come from the Japanese census which happens every five years. I proceed three ways to fill in missing variables:

Linear interpolation
: for data available only for years 2015 and 2020, mainly the ones from the census, I compute the linear line coefficients between those two variables and complete the missing variables depending on the year.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
********************************************************************************
* FILLING IN MISSING VALUES

* Linear interpolation
* Those for which 2015 and 2020 are available
foreach var of varlist pop_total median_age lifeexp_male lifeexp_female pop_u15 pop_o65 did_pop foreigners households pop_total2 employed unemployed labor_force skilled_workers selfemp_with selfemp_without commute_in commute_out {

	* Create base
	gen `var'_base = .
	bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_base = `var'[1]

	* Linear interpolation coefficients
	gen `var'_delta = .
	bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_delta = (`var'[6] / `var'[1]) ^ (1/5)

	* Interpolation computation
	gen `var'_linear = .
	forvalues y = 2015/2023 {
		local t = `y' - 2015
		replace `var'_linear = `var'_base * (`var'_delta ^ `t') if year == `y'
	}

	* Drop temporary var
	drop `var'_delta
	drop `var'_base
}
```

Regression
: this method is based on the previous one, but more than two years are available.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
* Regression
gen inmigrants_reg = inmigrants
gen outmigrants_reg = outmigrants
foreach var in inmigrants outmigrants {

    gen yhat = .

    levelsof areacode, local(areas)

    foreach a of local areas {
        count if areacode == `a' & !missing(year, `var')
        
        if r(N) >= 2 {
            regress `var' year if areacode == `a'
            predict temp if areacode == `a'
            
            replace yhat = round(temp) if areacode == `a'
            drop temp
        }
        else {
            display "Skipping areacode `a' for `var' (not enough data)"
        }
    }

    replace `var'_reg = yhat if missing(`var')
    drop yhat
}
```

Nearest value
: For the rest of the variables, if a observation is missing, I take the value of the previous year. For example, if some city had 2 libraries in 2015 and 2016 is N.A, then I suppose I also had 2 libraries in 2016.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
* Nearest values
foreach var of varlist comm_centers libraries hospitals physicians dentists  {
	gen `var'_near = `var'
	bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_near = `var'_near[_n+1] if missing(`var'_near[1])
	bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_near = `var'_near[_n-1] if missing(`var'_near) 
}
```

Simple mean
: I simply compute the mean for available years and fill it the remaining with it.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
* Simple mean computation
foreach var of varlist fin_power {
	bysort areacode (year): egen `var'_mean = mean(`var')
}
```

Finally, I create new variables more suitable for analysis.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
********************************************************************************
* CREATE RATIOS & VARIABLES

* Migration
gen inmigration_rate = inmigrants_reg / pop_total_linear 
gen outmigration_rate = outmigrants_reg / pop_total_linear
gen net_migration_rate = inmigrants_reg / outmigrants_reg
gen net_migration = inmigrants_reg - outmigrants_reg
gen commutation_rate = commute_in_linear / commute_out_linear

* Composition
gen pop_o65_share = pop_o65_linear / pop_total_linear
gen pop_u15_share = pop_u15_linear / pop_total_linear
gen foreigners_share = foreigners_linear / pop_total_linear
gen skilled_workers_share = skilled_workers_linear / pop_total2_linear

* Labour
gen employement_rate = employed_linear / pop_total2_linear
gen unemployment_rate = unemployed_linear / pop_total2_linear

* Health
gen hospitals_pc = hospitals_near / pop_total_linear
gen dentists_pc = dentists_near / pop_total_linear
gen physicians_pc = physicians_near / pop_total_linear

* Culture
gen libraries_pc = libraries_near / pop_total_linear
```

Let's save this cleaned dataset:

```{stata}
#| eval: false
save "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/estat.dta", replace
```

## MERGING

Now the data are pro-processed, they can be merged easily together. I decide to go with `Stata` from now on, expect when I will compute distances later.

```{stata}
#| eval: false
********************************************************************************
********************************************************************************
************ MERGING DATA ************
********************************************************************************
********************************************************************************

********************************************************************************
* LOAD THE MAIN DATASET
import parquet using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/liffull.parquet", clear

* Create areacode to match with e-stat data
gen areacode = string(prefecture) + string(city_district)
destring areacode, replace

********************************************************************************
* COMBINE WITH E-STAT DATASET
merge m:1 areacode year using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/estat.dta", keep(match) nogenerate

* COMBINE WITH MACRO DATASET
merge m:1 year week using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clena/macro.dta", nogenerate

********************************************************************************
* COMBINE WITH JMA DATASET
* Since the earthquake records are not that numerous after selection,
* I decide to go manually to avoid coding errors:

* I create an index for the records
gen eq_index = .

* Retrieving records dates from the eq_records dataset
* Each of the following is ordered the same way as in the eq_records
local dates ///
20125 20136 20221 20230 20233 20238 20243 20279 ///
20282 20343 20467 20558 20559 20560 20562 20563 ///
20590 20621 20662 20697 20723 20748 20780 20816 ///
20878 20990 20995 21001 21011 21070 21098 21244 ///
21283 21288 21316 21329 21353 21372 21433 21462 ///
21552 21601 21679 21694 21718 21765 21902 21987 ///
22091 22162 22241 22261 22267 22270 22324 22359 ///
22401 22539 22559 22560 22617 22623 22649 22667 ///
22720 22722 22754 22787 22815 22816 22868 22920 ///
22939 23066 23135 23141 23143 23152 23156 23172

* If a publication starts in the next 7 days following an earthquake,
* then it takes the index value associated to that earthquake
local i = 1
foreach d of local dates {
    di "date = `d'   id = `i'"
    replace eq_index = `i' if date > `d' & date < (`d' + 7)
    local ++i
}

* Now merge according to the index
merge m:1 index_10 using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/records.dta", keep(master match) nogenerate
```

## VARIABLE CREATION

This section shows the variables I create to use later in the analysis part.

I use python to compute the distance between each observation and each earthquake.

do-file contains the new variables I will use in the analysis part.


## VISUALISATION

### Basic visuals

Let's first visualize the different datasets seperately. To get a better intuition of the following study, let's visualize risk maps:

[![2020 SHM from J-SHIS website](images/shm_2020_style.png){fig-alt="Blue"}](https://www.j-shis.bosai.go.jp/map/?lang=en)

### Descritptive statistics

### Pre-analysis visualization

Here I take a step forward to analysis by taking a look at correlation.

## ANALYSIS

#### Treatment definition

#### Regression tables

## OLD

#### J-SHIS Hazard Maps

Many parameters are available, and have an impact on what the hazard maps look like. I decide to focus on columns 02 and 04 since there are presented as the most common for public hazard map information to the public:

```{r}
#| echo: false

df <- tibble(
  Column = sprintf("%02d", 1:23),
  Header = c(
    "CODE",
    "T30_I45_PS", "T30_I50_PS", "T30_I55_PS", "T30_I60_PS",
    "T30_P03_SI", "T30_P03_BV", "T30_P03_SV",
    "T30_P06_SI", "T30_P06_BV", "T30_P06_SV",
    "T50_P02_SI", "T50_P02_BV", "T50_P02_SV",
    "T50_P05_SI",
    "T50_P05_BV", "T50_P05_SV",
    "T50_P10_SI", "T50_P10_BV", "T50_P10_SV",
    "T50_P39_SI", "T50_P39_BV", "T50_P39_SV"
  ),
  Format = c(
    "%10-11c",
    rep("%9.6e", 4),
    "%3.1f", "%9.6e", "%9.6e",
    "%3.1f", "%9.6e", "%9.6e",
    "%3.1f", "%9.6e", "%9.6e",
    "%3.1f",
    "%9.6e", "%9.6e",
    "%3.1f", "%9.6e", "%9.6e",
    "%3.1f", "%9.6e", "%9.6e"
  ),
  Explanation = c(
    "250m mesh code",
    "Probability of exceedance [IJMA >=5-Lower] within 30 years",
    "Probability of exceedance [IJMA >=5-Upper] within 30 years",
    "Probability of exceedance [IJMA >=6-Lower] within 30 years",
    "Probability of exceedance [IJMA >=6-Upper] within 30 years",
    "IJMA for a 3% probability of exceedance within 30 years",
    "PBV for a 3% probability of exceedance within 30 years (cm/s)",
    "PGV for a 3% probability of exceedance within 30 years (cm/s)",
    "IJMA for a 6% probability of exceedance within 30 years",
    "PBV for a 6% probability of exceedance within 30 years (cm/s)",
    "PGV for a 6% probability of exceedance within 30 years (cm/s)",
    "IJMA for a 2% probability of exceedance within 50 years",
    "PBV for a 2% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "PGV for a 2% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "IJMA for a 5% probability of exceedance within 50 years",
    "PBV for a 5% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "PGV for a 5% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "IJMA for a 10% probability of exceedance within 50 years",
    "PBV for a 10% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "PGV for a 10% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "IJMA for a 39% probability of exceedance within 50 years",
    "PBV for a 39% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)",
    "PGV for a 39% probability of exceedance within 50 years (cm/s)"
  )
)

df |>
  kable(caption = "Variables of the J-SHIS shapefy file") |>
  scroll_box(
    height = "170px",
    width = "100%"
  )
```

This is what it looks like:

![](images/shm_2016_final.png){width="789"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Back to Home](index.html)
